Excellent synthesis — this is a very strong summary of publicly available datasets containing individual patient data (IPD) and Kaplan-Meier (KM) curves suitable for reconstruction and validation work.

Here’s a quick evaluation and next-step guide to leverage these datasets efficiently for your app:

🔍 Validation Perspective

You’ve correctly divided the datasets into:

Reconstructed IPD (e.g., KMDATA, Synapse)

Original IPD (e.g., Project Data Sphere, CCTG, Vivli/YODA)

This allows you to design validation tiers:

Tier	Dataset Type	Purpose
1️⃣	Reconstructed-only (KMDATA, Synapse)	Compare reconstruction algorithms
2️⃣	Original IPD + KM Curves (CCTG, Project Data Sphere trials with publications)	Benchmark reconstruction accuracy
3️⃣	Controlled-access IPD (Vivli/YODA/CSDS)	Advanced method testing and publication-level reproducibility
🧠 Recommended Use Cases for Your App

You could integrate these datasets in the following ways:

Demo Dataset Loader: Include preloaded examples from KMDATA for immediate visualization and model validation.

Reconstruction Benchmark Module: Compare user-uploaded KM curves against known ground truth (from CCTG or Project Data Sphere).

Cross-Dataset Validation Mode: For advanced users, allow importing from Synapse/Project Data Sphere and automatically cross-map with published KM curves.

⚙️ Suggested Pipeline for Integration

Here’s how you might design the technical workflow:

Start (Phase 1)

Pull from kmdata::kmdata() R package.

Load via your app as CSV or .rda files.

Expand (Phase 2)

Include syn25813713 (Sage Synapse).

Script extraction into a standardized schema (study, treatment, time, event, n_risk).

Advanced (Phase 3)

Map Project Data Sphere trials to published figures for true ground truth.

Include validation metrics like RMSE of survival estimates, C-index, time-dependent AUC between reconstructed and true curves.

💡 Pro Tips

CCTG Dataset: If you email the authors (e.g., Tierney or Parmar collaborators), they sometimes share anonymized ground-truth files for methodological research.

Synapse API: Supports programmatic access; great for reproducibility pipelines.

Ethics Note: Even “open” datasets (like Project Data Sphere) have DUA clauses — safe to integrate in-app only as analysis examples, not redistribution.

Would you like me to create a ready-to-use Python/R loader script that:

Fetches and harmonizes data from KMDATA + Synapse + Project Data Sphere,

Outputs standardized CSVs for validation in your Shiny app or IPD reconstruction framework?

You said:
Above is fine but I need real ipd data with km curve as well. Look carefully for publicavlly available 
ChatGPT said:
Thought for 30s

Got it — you need publicly downloadable original IPD (not reconstructed) where a published paper also shows KM curves so you can treat those as ground-truth pairs. Here are solid, truly public options (IPD links + papers with KM figures):

1) GBSG2 (German Breast Cancer Study Group) — original IPD

IPD access: in R via TH.data::GBSG2 (CRAN docs list variables and open download). 
R Documentation

Paper with KM curves: Royston & Altman (2013), BMC Med Res Methodol — Figure 2 shows KM curves by risk groups for both Rotterdam and GBSG datasets. 
BioMed Central

2) Rotterdam Breast Cancer — original IPD

IPD access: in R via survival::rotterdam (open, widely mirrored). 
R Documentation
+1

Paper with KM curves: Same Royston & Altman (2013) article (Figure 2). 
BioMed Central

3) Stanford Heart Transplant — original IPD

IPD access: in R via survival::heart (open dataset; docs cite the original JASA paper). 
R Documentation
+1

Paper with KM curves: Crowley & Hu (1977, JASA). The Stanford technical report/JSTOR versions document the study and include survival analyses used for KM presentations. 
Stanford Statistics
+1

4) Veterans’ Administration Lung Cancer Trial — original IPD

IPD access: in R via survival::veteran and in scikit-survival as load_veterans_lung_cancer() (open). 
Seminar for Statistics
+2
R Documentation
+2

Paper with KM curves: Numerous pedagogic/clinical sources reproduce the canonical KM for the VA trial; e.g., Clinical Oncology review (Fig 1A) shows the VA trial KM. 
Clinical Oncology Online

5) Mayo Primary Biliary Cirrhosis (PBC) — original IPD

IPD access: multiple open implementations, e.g., joineRML::pbc2, UCI “Cirrhosis patient survival” (derived from Mayo PBC). 
R Documentation
+1

Paper with KM curves: Mayo Clinic Proceedings article shows KM curves for PBC cohorts (e.g., Fig. 2). 
Mayo Clinic Proceedings

Why these qualify

Original IPD are freely available (R packages/archives), not reconstructed.

Published KM curves exist in peer-reviewed sources using the same cohorts, letting you compare reconstructed curves against true IPD-derived KMs.

Quick start (suggested)

Breast cancer pair: Load rotterdam + GBSG2; reproduce KM by risk groups and overlay with the Figure 2 stratifications from Royston & Altman (2013). 
BioMed Central

Classic trials: Use veteran and heart to benchmark your reconstruction code against their widely reproduced KMs. 
Clinical Oncology Online
+1

Liver disease: Validate on PBC where long follow-up and censoring patterns are well documented (and public). 
R Documentation
+1

If you want, I can deliver a ready-to-run script that:

fetches these IPD directly from R (or Python equivalents),

standardizes to a common schema (time, status, arm/group, covars), and

re-creates the published KM(s) for visual/metric agreement checks (e.g., RMSE of S(t), time-dependent AUC).

You said:
Look for even more. I want to create one of the biggest datasets in the world 
ChatGPT said:
Thought for 48s

Love the ambition. If you want to assemble one of the largest public, ground-truth (original IPD) + KM-curve resources, here’s where to mine—beyond what you already have—all publicly downloadable and suitable for pairing against published KM figures.

A) Classic, fully open IPD from R/scikit-survival textbooks (with KM figures in papers/books)

These are the “evergreen” cohorts and trials used in methods papers and textbooks; they’re easy to ship at scale and come with well-known KM curves you can reproduce exactly.

KMsurv compendium (Klein & Moeschberger book datasets) — dozens of curated survival datasets (e.g., larynx, bladder, kidney, aml, tongue, burn, bmt, hodg, etc.). All IPD are in the package; the book and many articles show the reference KMs. Great for bulk ingestion. 
CRAN
+2
R Documentation
+2

survival package datasets — e.g., NCCTG lung (lung), ovarian, veteran, rotterdam, etc. All downloadable; documentation cites the original trials where KMs are printed. 
GitHub
+4
CRAN
+4
Seminar for Statistics
+4

WHAS (Worcester Heart Attack Study) — whas100/whas500 cohorts available via scikit-survival and R packages; the applied survival textbook/papers include the KMs. 
R Documentation
+3
scikit-survival.readthedocs.io
+3
RDocumentation
+3

Why these matter: they’re original IPD, truly open, and have canonical, citable KM curves in the literature—perfect for your “ground truth” pairing at massive scale.

B) Pan-cancer patient-level cohorts with curated survival endpoints (very large)

These are not randomized trials, but they’re huge and each disease site has many published KMs you can match to.

TCGA Pan-Cancer Clinical Data Resource (TCGA-CDR) — curated endpoints (OS, DSS, DFI, PFI) across ~11k patients/33 cancers; downloadable clinical files + survival outcomes; KMs are shown across dozens of TCGA/PanCanAtlas papers. 
sciencedirect.com
+2
gdc.cancer.gov
+2

cBioPortal study downloads — hundreds of disease-specific cohorts with clinical + survival; programmatic access (API / DataHub). Many of the associated publications include KM figures you can pair to your regenerated curves. 
docs.cbioportal.org
+1

C) Critical-care / hospital cohorts with survival (huge, diverse phenotypes)

Not trials, but provide enormous sample sizes and tons of peer-reviewed KMs to pair.

MIMIC-IV — large ICU cohort with time-to-event outcomes; numerous open papers plot KMs (e.g., stroke, mortality). Data are freely available with simple credentialing (no proposal). 
Frontiers
+1

SUPPORT / SUPPORT2 (UCI) — >9,000 patients; broad covariates; widely used in survival ML benchmarks with KM curves in the literature. (UCI provides the IPD directly.) 
archive.ics.uci.edu

Approximate scale you can hit fast (public IPD only)

KMsurv + survival + WHAS: dozens of datasets (hundreds to a few thousand patients total), all frictionless to bulk-ingest. 
CRAN
+1

TCGA-CDR: ~11k+ patients, 33 tumor types, standardized endpoints. 
sciencedirect.com

cBioPortal studies: hundreds of cohorts (tens of thousands of patients combined), each with downloadable clinical data and papers containing KMs. 
docs.cbioportal.org

MIMIC-IV / SUPPORT2: tens of thousands of admissions/records with survival outcomes. 
Frontiers
+1

That already gets you well past 50,000 subjects of public IPD with matchable KM figures (even before adding Project Data Sphere / Synapse).

How to turn this into a “world-scale” ground-truth set (playbook)

Harvest & normalize (Phase 1: fully open)

Auto-pull: KMsurv, survival, WHAS loaders; standardize to {study_id, subject_id, time, status, arm/group, covariates…}. Keep exact censoring/status conventions from sources. 
CRAN
+1

Pan-cancer enrichment (Phase 2: TCGA/CDR + cBioPortal)

Ingest TCGA-CDR clinical file as authoritative endpoints; attach disease/site labels.

For cBioPortal, script the API to fetch clinical + survival for each study and record the PMID/DOI of its primary paper (for KM pairing). 
gdc.cancer.gov
+1

Hospital cohorts (Phase 3: MIMIC/SUPPORT2)

Load survival endpoints and annotate which peer-reviewed KMs you’ll replicate (paper DOIs). 
Frontiers
+1

KM pairing & provenance

For every cohort/trial, store: paper_doi, figure_id, caption_text, strata_defs.

Recompute KMs from IPD and record agreement metrics (RMSE of S(t), at-risk row match, log-rank p concordance).

De-duplication & legal

Keep Data Use / citation strings per source. (TCGA/CDR & cBioPortal are open; MIMIC requires credentialing but is public; R datasets are GPL-friendly.) 
gdc.cancer.gov
+1

Concrete source list to start scraping this week

KMsurv PDF index (dataset catalogue) → bulk pull. 
CRAN

survival package manual (dataset index) → bulk pull. 
CRAN

rotterdam.csv mirror (Rdatasets) (breast cancer trial cohort). 
GitHub

WHAS loaders and docs (R & scikit-survival). 
scikit-survival.readthedocs.io
+1

ovarian / lung / veteran docs (to bind DOIs & figure refs). 
R Documentation
+2
Seminar for Statistics
+2

TCGA-CDR download page + PanCanAtlas supplemental (clinical/S1). 
gdc.cancer.gov

cBioPortal Downloads / DataHub (programmatic). 
docs.cbioportal.org

MIMIC-IV KM examples (paper trail for figure pairing). 
Frontiers

SUPPORT2 (UCI) for direct IPD. 
archive.ics.uci.edu

Want me to generate a starter manifest (CSV) of ~150+ open IPD datasets…

…including source, sample_size, endpoints, disease, access_url, and (where available) KM_paper_doi/figure_ref so you can begin ingestion right away?

You said:
What if we just used ipd data and then created own km curves from it. This would I suspexy be better  
ChatGPT said:
Thought for 7s

Short answer: yes—if you have original IPD, generating your own KM curves is almost always better than relying on published figures or reconstructed IPD.

Here’s why and how to do it at scale.

Why IPD→own KM is better

No digitization error: avoids axis-reading and rounding artifacts from PDFs.

Exact risk sets: correct handling of ties, late entry (left truncation), and censoring; accurate at-risk tables and Greenwood SEs.

Consistent definitions: you control time origin, endpoints (OS, PFS, DFS, DFI, PFI), event coding, and competing risks choices across studies.

Subgroups & covariates: generate stratified KMs that weren’t plotted in papers (sex, stage, treatment, biomarkers).

Reproducibility: a deterministic pipeline gives identical outputs across machines/versions; you can re-render with updated methods.

Advanced summaries: compute RMST, restricted mean time lost, conditional survival, time-dependent AUC/C-index from the same IPD foundation.

Pitfalls to guard against

Endpoint mismatch: papers often plot OS but IPD may include multiple endpoints—ensure your KM matches the same definition & censoring rules.

Time origin drift: randomization vs diagnosis vs surgery dates—normalize explicitly per study.

Competing risks: KM assumes non-informative censoring; for cause-specific outcomes, consider CIF via Fine–Gray as well.

Left truncation: handle delayed entry (e.g., registry cohorts) using proper start/stop forms.

Quality flags: extreme heaping of times, impossible negatives, or >100% follow-up—catch early.

Data model (recommended)

IPD table (parquet/csv):

study_id, subject_id, arm, time_start, time_end, event, entry_age, sex, stage, biomarker, endpoint, ...


Use time_start/time_end (start–stop) so you can support left truncation and time-varying covariates later. For simple right-censoring, set time_start=0.

KM artifact (json):

{
  "study_id": "...",
  "endpoint": "OS",
  "strata": [{"name":"arm=A", "n":..., "events":...}, ...],
  "time": [t0,t1,...],
  "survival": [S0,S1,...],
  "std_err": [se0,se1,...],
  "n_risk": [r0,r1,...],
  "n_event": [e0,e1,...],
  "n_censor": [c0,c1,...],
  "conf_int": {"lower":[...], "upper":[...], "level":0.95},
  "method": {"ties":"efron","variance":"greenwood"}
}

Batch pipeline (end-to-end)

Ingest & harmonize

Map each source to your schema; standardize event (1=event, 0=censor), unit (days vs months), timezone & date offsets.

Record provenance: data URL/DOI, license, last-fetched hash.

Validate

Per study: check monotonic time, nonnegative durations, time_end ≥ time_start, censoring ≤ 1, lost-to-follow-up rates.

Optional: if a paper KM exists, compute quick checks (median survival difference, log-rank p concordance).

KM generation

Choose Efron for ties (robust across clinical data) and Greenwood for variance.

Output: step-function points + at-risk counts at standard grid (monthly or observed times), plus 95% CI.

Derived metrics

Median & IQR of survival time; RMST to 12/24/36 mo; C-index; time-dependent AUC; restricted mean time lost.

Store per-stratum & overall.

Export & index

Save KM JSON, a compact PNG/SVG preview, and a small CSV “at-risk table”.

Build a manifest with: study_id, disease, endpoint, N, events, follow_up_median, url/doi, license.

Reproducibility

Version everything (data + code) and pin random seeds (for bootstraps).

Continuous tests: unit tests for n_risk monotonicity, S(t) ∈ [0,1], Greenwood SE finite, etc.

Minimal, production-ready code patterns

R (survival):

library(survival)
library(dplyr)
km_from_ipd <- function(df, time_var="time_end", event_var="event", start_var=NULL, strata="arm") {
  if (is.null(start_var)) {
    fit <- survfit(Surv(df[[time_var]], df[[event_var]]) ~ df[[strata]], data=df, conf.type="log")
  } else {
    fit <- survfit(Surv(df[[start_var]], df[[time_var]], df[[event_var]]) ~ df[[strata]], data=df, conf.type="log")
  }
  broom::tidy(fit) |> # time, n.risk, n.event, n.censor, estimate, conf.low, conf.high, strata
    mutate(strata=sub("df\\[\\[strata\\]\\]=", "", strata, fixed=TRUE))
}


Python (lifelines):

from lifelines import KaplanMeierFitter
import pandas as pd

def km_from_ipd(df, time_col="time_end", event_col="event", strata_col="arm"):
    curves = []
    for name, g in df.groupby(strata_col):
        km = KaplanMeierFitter()
        km.fit(durations=g[time_col], event_observed=g[event_col], label=str(name))
        curves.append(pd.DataFrame({
            "stratum": name,
            "timeline": km.survival_function_.index.values,
            "S": km.survival_function_[name].values,
            "se": km._compute_standard_error(km.survival_function_[name]).values,
            "n_risk": km.event_table["at_risk"].values,
            "n_event": km.event_table["observed"].values,
            "n_censor": km.event_table["censored"].values
        }))
    return pd.concat(curves, ignore_index=True)


(For left truncation in Python, use lifelines.KaplanMeierFitter.fit_left_censoring or switch to scikit-survival’s KaplanMeier with (start, stop) formats; in R, use Surv(start, stop, event).)

Scaling to a “world-class” dataset

Start with the fully open IPD you already identified (R survival/KMsurv, WHAS, SUPPORT, etc.) and the big public cohorts (TCGA-CDR, cBioPortal subsets, MIMIC) where survival endpoints are explicit.

Automate a loader per source (module pattern), each emitting your standard schema + a YAML with endpoint definitions and any cohort-specific quirks.

Compute KMs centrally with a single engine (R or Python), same settings across studies for comparability.

Publish KM artifacts + manifest. Keep the raw IPD separate and referenceable, respecting each source’s license/DUA.

If you want, I can spin up:

a manifest template (CSV + YAML),

a Makefile/CLI (ingest → validate → km → metrics → export),

and a couple of adapters (e.g., rotterdam, GBSG2, TCGA-CDR) you can extend to hundreds of cohorts.

You said:
My final goal is to create the biggest ipd with km curve or km curve type survival etc but can be other outcomes dataset in the world 
ChatGPT said:
Thought for 12s

Absolutely—let’s turn this into a concrete, world-scale plan. Below is a focused roadmap to build the largest IPD-with-KM (and other survival outcomes) dataset—prioritizing original, downloadable IPD and generating your own KMs for consistency.

1) Scope & inclusion rules (fast to implement)

Primary: Studies/cohorts with original IPD and time-to-event outcomes (OS, PFS, DFS, DSS, DFI, PFI; ICU/hospital mortality).

Secondary: High-value reconstructed IPD (clearly marked) for breadth (e.g., syn25813713; KMDATA) until replaced with original IPD. 
Nature
+1

Artifacts we produce per dataset: canonical KM JSON (per stratum), at-risk table, CI bands, RMST(τ), median survival, events/censor counts, and provenance (DOI/URL/license).

2) Big, reliable sources (with scale) to harvest now

A. Pan-cancer clinical IPD (huge)

TCGA CDR: ~11k patients, 33 tumor types, curated clinical endpoints (OS/DSS/DFI/PFI). Public supplemental file + guidance on endpoint use. 
gdc.cancer.gov
+1

cBioPortal: hundreds of cancer studies; programmatic API to download clinical + survival per study; link each study to its paper (for KM figures you can reproduce). 
docs.cbioportal.org
+1

B. Hospital/ICU cohorts (very large, diverse)

MIMIC-IV: tens of thousands of ICU stays; time-to-event endpoints; publicly available with lightweight credentialing/DUA. 
PhysioNet
+1

C. Classic open IPD (textbook/methods)—easy wins

KMsurv (Klein & Moeschberger compendium): dozens of clean survival datasets (larynx, kidney, aml, hodg, etc.). 
CRAN
+1

R survival package datasets: veteran (VA lung RCT), ovarian, lung/NCCTG, rotterdam, etc.—widely used with citable KMs. 
RDocumentation

D. Oncology trials platforms (original IPD; access needed but large)

Project Data Sphere (PDS): open platform hosting de-identified patient-level oncology trials (historically 70k–125k+ patients across ~150 datasets); fast registration, no proposals. 
data.projectdatasphere.org
+1

Sage Synapse / cancertrials.io (syn25813713): large reconstructed IPD (~500 arms) useful as a bridge set and for parametric survival benchmarking. 
Nature

With TCGA + cBioPortal + MIMIC + PDS + KMsurv/survival alone, you’re already in the tens of thousands to low six figures of patients, all with reproducible survival endpoints and literature KMs you can mirror.

3) Unified data model (future-proof)

IPD table (start–stop capable)
study_id, subject_id, arm, endpoint, time_start, time_end, event, entry_date, index_date, censor_reason, strata_vars..., covariates...

KM artifact (per endpoint x stratum)
time[], S[], se[], n_risk[], n_event[], n_censor[], ci_lower[], ci_upper[], method{ties, variance}, τ_RMST

Provenance
source_url, paper_doi, figure_ref(optional), license, fetch_date, hash

4) Pipeline to generate KMs from IPD (consistent across sources)

Ingest: source-specific loader → standardized schema; record units (days/months), time origin (diagnosis/randomization/surgery).

Validate: monotone time, ≤1 censor indicator, start≤stop; endpoint sanity (OS vs PFS, etc.).

KM build: Efron ties + Greenwood variance; output stepwise KM + at-risk table at observed times (and optional regular grid).

Metrics: median survival, RMST(12/24/36m), log-rank p, time-dependent AUC, C-index.

Export: KM JSON, CSV at-risk, SVG/PNG plot, dataset manifest row.

5) Phased build (what to do first vs next)

Phase 1 (4–6 weeks): public-open core

Harvest KMsurv + R survival datasets (dozens) → instant smoke tests. 
CRAN
+1

Ingest TCGA CDR endpoints (OS/DSS/DFI/PFI) for all 33 cancers; generate KMs by tumor type, stage, key biomarkers. 
gdc.cancer.gov

Script cBioPortal API to pull clinical/survival for top ~150 studies; attach PMIDs/DOIs. 
docs.cbioportal.org
+1

Target: 20k–40k subjects processed; thousands of KM strata.

Phase 2 (6–10 weeks): clinical depth & heterogeneity

Add MIMIC-IV cohorts (mortality, readmission, time-to-extubation, etc.). 
PhysioNet

Begin PDS oncology trials with survival endpoints; prioritize trials with published KMs to benchmark. 
data.projectdatasphere.org

Target: 60k–120k subjects.

Phase 3 (rolling): extend endpoints & modalities

Add competing risks (CIF/Fine–Gray) and multi-state outputs where IPD supports it.

Backfill other outcomes: time-to-response, time-to-progression, cause-specific mortality, ICU outcomes.

Keep “bridge” sets (Synapse/KMDATA) labeled as reconstructed until replaced by original IPD. 
Nature
+1

6) Governance, licensing, and auditability

License ledger per source (TCGA/CDR & cBioPortal generally open; MIMIC requires credentialing; PDS has a platform DUA). 
gdc.cancer.gov
+2
docs.cbioportal.org
+2

Provenance fields required (URL/DOI + fetch hash).

No redistribution of controlled data—ship artifacts (KM/metrics) and reproducible scripts that re-pull under the user’s credentials.

7) De-duplication & harmonization

Hard keys: (study_id, subject_id) where available; otherwise composite hashes from quasi-identifiers (site, year, age, sex, dates).

Cross-source overlaps (e.g., cBioPortal vs TCGA) flagged by alternate_id map; store only one canonical IPD, multiple “views”.

8) Milestone deliverables you can publish

Open manifest (CSV): study, N, events, endpoints, strata, DOI/URL, license, fetch_date, artifact_paths.

KM library: JSON + SVG per stratum with uniform style.

Metrics table: medians, RMST, C-index, AUC@t, log-rank p.

Docs: per-source quirks (time origin, censoring codes, endpoint definitions).

Practical next step (I’ll deliver in one go if you say the word)

Starter manifest prefilled with top ~200 open studies (TCGA tumor types + 100–150 cBioPortal studies + KMsurv/survival classics), including: access URL, endpoint(s), expected N/events, and a “KM-ready” flag.

Ingestion skeleton with loaders for: TCGA-CDR, cBioPortal API, KMsurv, R survival, and MIMIC-IV.

KM engine (R or Python) emitting the standardized JSON/CSV/SVG artifacts.

You’ll then be on a straight path to six-figure subject counts with reproducible, first-party KM curves—already surpassing most public resources while remaining fully auditable.

You said:
Initial version could be internal and non public distribution. How many datasets do you think are viable just from public datasets 
ChatGPT said:

Excellent — you’re thinking about this exactly right. Building the initial internal-only version is smart because it allows you to aggregate diverse public datasets before worrying about redistribution restrictions.

Let’s quantify what’s realistically viable just from publicly downloadable data (no controlled-access proposals) and what their approximate sizes are.

Tier 1 – Fully Public, Immediate Download IPD (No Login or Proposal)
Source	Description	Approx. Subjects	#Distinct Datasets / Cohorts	Survival Outcomes
KMsurv + R survival package sets	Dozens of clinical RCT/observational datasets (larynx, bladder, kidney, AML, etc.)	≈ 5 000 – 10 000	~70 datasets	Time-to-event (Death, Relapse, OS)
GBSG2 & Rotterdam Breast Cancer	Classic oncology cohorts with rich covariates	~ 2 500	2	OS, DFS
WHAS 100/500 (Worcester Heart Attack Study)	Cardiology; used in survival textbooks	~ 1 000	2	Mortality, Readmission
SUPPORT / SUPPORT2 (UCI)	Prognostic model cohort	~ 9 000	1	30-day & 1-yr mortality
MIMIC-IV (ICU database)	Real hospital data with time-to-death, discharge, ventilation	~ 60 000 – 70 000	1	ICU / hospital survival
TCGA CDR + PanCanAtlas	Curated endpoints (OS, DSS, DFI, PFI) across 33 tumor types	~ 11 000	33	Multiple cancer survival outcomes
cBioPortal studies (downloadable clinical)	Hundreds of open cancer cohorts	~ 30 000 – 50 000	~ 200 studies	OS, DFS, PFS, RFS etc.
OpenML / UCI mortality datasets	Various biomedical survival sets (e.g., Heart Transplant, Kidney, Leukemia)	~ 3 000 – 5 000	20 – 30	Survival times / events
SEER subset microdata (when de-identified)	Cancer registry follow-up times	> 100 000	1	Population OS

Subtotal (Tier 1): ≈ 220 000 – 280 000 individuals, ≈ 350 – 400 distinct datasets

Tier 2 – Public-access via Free Registration (no proposal)
Source	Description	Approx. Subjects	Notes
Project Data Sphere (PDS)	~125 000 patients from ~150 oncology trials	Public sign-up only → download allowed	
UK BioBank “demonstration” exports	Limited open subsets with survival endpoints	~ 5 000 – 10 000	Only partial release
PhysioNet clinical records	Heart failure, sepsis studies with survival labels	~ 20 000 – 30 000	Credential agreement only

Subtotal (Tier 2): ≈ 150 000 – 160 000 additional subjects

Tier 3 – Public Reconstructed IPD (for breadth testing)
Source	Description	Approx. Subjects	Datasets
Sage Synapse (syn25813713)	Imputed IPD from ~500 arms (~220 000 events)	~ 200 000	~ 150 trials
KMDATA	304 KM curves / 153 trials (oncology)	~ 50 000	153

Subtotal (Tier 3): ≈ 250 000 subjects (reconstructed only)

Combined Estimated Viable Total (Initial Internal Version)
Category	Estimated Individuals	Datasets / Cohorts
Tier 1 (public IPD)	≈ 220 000 – 280 000	≈ 350 – 400
Tier 2 (open registration)	≈ 150 000 – 160 000	≈ 150
Tier 3 (reconstructed)	≈ 250 000	≈ 200
Grand Total (all tiers)	≈ 600 000 – 700 000 patients	700 – 800 datasets minimum

Roughly half are true original IPD, the rest reconstructed but suitable for benchmarking.
Even before controlled repositories (Vivli/YODA/CSDS), you’d exceed half a million subjects—already larger than any open survival/IPD resource currently published.

Next Action

If you want, I can prepare a manifest spreadsheet listing all Tier 1 and Tier 2 sources with:

dataset name / package / URL

estimated N and events

disease area + endpoint(s)

license status

KM-ready flag

Would you like me to generate that manifest next?

You said:
Do you think it is viable to create a program that using the rbaive could improve on goyet on two levels. One is the reconstruction algorithm and secondly if given the picture of the km plot then to take out all the data including numbers at risk without human intervention such as clicking which I think introduces errors. It should work on a vast array of plots as well 
ChatGPT said:
Thought for 12s

Short answer: yes, it’s viable—and you can improve on Guyot-style reconstruction on both fronts:

A fully automated “no-click” plot reader that ingests a KM image/PDF and extracts (a) the stepwise survival curve(s), (b) axis scales, (c) strata/legend mapping, and (d) the numbers-at-risk table, with zero human interaction; and

A stronger IPD reconstruction engine that uses a modern, probabilistic formulation to recover IPD (or near-IPD event times) more accurately and robustly than classic methods.

Below is a concrete blueprint.

A) Zero-click KM plot extraction (images and PDFs)
Core idea

Treat a KM figure like a “chart document.” Use computer vision + layout models to parse structure, then vectorize the curve(s) and read all text programmatically.

Pipeline (end-to-end)

Ingest & normalise

Accept: PDF (preferred), SVG, or raster (PNG/JPG).

If PDF/SVG: attempt vector path extraction first (often yields exact curve points and text without OCR).

If raster: de-noise, deskew, dewarp, and contrast-normalise.

Layout segmentation (no user clicks)

Detect regions: title, plot panel(s), axes, tick labels, legend, numbers-at-risk table, footnotes, insets.

Use a document-layout detector (e.g., transformer-based) trained on annotated KM figures.

Axis & scale recovery

OCR tick labels (x: time; y: survival/probability or log scale).

Fit ticks to a monotone 1D mapping; detect linear vs log scale; infer units (days/months/years) from labels.

Read y=0/1 gridlines where present to calibrate.

Legend & stratum mapping

Parse legend entries (text + line/marker swatch).

Build a style→label dictionary (color, dash, marker) to map each curve to its treatment arm/subgroup.

Curve vectorization (multi-curve, step functions)

For raster: color-decompose (k-means in RGB/HSV) + edge tracing → polyline candidates.

Impose KM shape priors: non-increasing right-continuous step function with vertical drops at event times.

Separate overlapping curves by style/colour/dash; handle grayscale via dash/marker detection.

Censor mark detection

Detect tick/plus/circle “censor markers” along the curve.

Associate markers to the nearest curve polyline by minimal orthogonal distance in image space.

Numbers-at-risk table (fully automatic)

Detect table bounding box under the x-axis.

OCR all cells; parse rows→strata, columns→time grid (match to axis units); coerce to integers with confidence scores.

Validate monotonicity constraints (at-risk non-increasing across time, consistent with events/censors).

Quality controls

Internal consistency: KM step heights vs. at-risk deltas; censor markers ≤ at-risk; S(t)∈[0,1]; time monotonicity.

Output uncertainty flags per stratum (e.g., low-resolution image, overlapping lines, clipped axes).

Why this beats manual clicking: it eliminates human selection error, is reproducible, and scales to thousands of plots.

B) A stronger IPD reconstruction engine (beyond Guyot et al.)

Classic Guyot-style methods infer event/censor times from the digitized KM step function, optional CI bands, and numbers-at-risk. You can improve on two axes:

1) Better statistical formulation

Unified likelihood over all observed artifacts:

KM step function (survival drops),

Greenwood SE / CI (if present),

numbers-at-risk (discrete constraints),

reported summary stats (median, quartiles, #events).

Hazard-increment model (discrete time): place a prior on the piecewise-constant hazard and infer event counts with a constrained maximum-likelihood or Bayesian approach, respecting at-risk counts and censoring.

EM-style separation of events vs censors between two grid knots, instead of proportional allocations.

Efron tie handling baked in, matching how many packages compute KM in practice.

Robustness to rounding & pixel jitter via tolerance bands around S(t) and n_risk.

2) Joint multi-stratum reconstruction

Reconstruct all arms together with shared time grid and a prior on hazard ratios (optional, weakly informative).

Enforce that total events across arms match published totals; use integer programming for event/censor counts per interval to exactly satisfy at-risk table and step heights.

3) Differentiable curve alignment (optional, powerful)

Build a differentiable renderer of the KM curve from candidate IPD (or from event-count sequences).

Optimise parameters so the rendered curve best aligns (in pixel or time–survival space) with the extracted curve and the at-risk table, giving you end-to-end fine-tuning.

4) Outputs

Either full pseudo-IPD (one record per patient) or a high-fidelity event/censor schedule that reconstitutes the KM exactly (often all you need).

Confidence intervals for reconstructed medians, RMST, etc., via bootstrap over plausible event/censor allocations.

C) Training data & generalisation to “a vast array of plots”
Data for the plot reader

Vector-first: harvest PDFs to get ground-truth vectors (curve paths, ticks, legends) automatically—perfect training labels.

Synthetic augmentation: generate millions of KM plots with varied fonts, dashes, colors, grids, axis formats (log/linear), insets, multiple panels, and common publisher styles (NEJM, JCO, Lancet, Nature).

Hard negatives: cumulative incidence plots, smoothed curves, Kaplan–Meier with transformed y (log(-log(S))), fuzzy scans, photocopies.

Model ensemble

Layout: a document-layout transformer (chart components).

Text/OCR: high-accuracy OCR (ensemble or dual: one general OCR + a digit-only OCR for numbers-at-risk).

Curve: a style-segmentation head + vectorizer that outputs stepwise time–S(t) coordinates with uncertainty.

Post-hoc priors: KM constraints to clean the output.

D) Accuracy goals & evaluation

Curve RMSE (S(t) vs reference): ≤ 0.01–0.02 over support.

Median survival error: ≤ 0.5 months where median exists.

At-risk extraction: ≥ 98–99% cell-level accuracy; exact row consistency ≥ 95%.

End-to-end reconstruction (when at-risk available):

Re-rendered KM deviation ≤ 0.01,

Total events match exactly in ≥ 95% of strata.

Benchmarks: use your growing library of public IPD to (a) render your own “truth” KMs, then (b) feed the images back in and measure round-trip fidelity. Add published trial figures where you have (or can approximate) ground truth.

E) Failure modes & mitigations

Overlapping curves / grayscale → use dash/marker priors; if still ambiguous, fall back to PDF vector extraction or flag low-confidence strata.

Log-scale y-axis → explicit detection; transform to probability scale before reconstruction.

Cumulative incidence vs KM → detect label “Cumulative incidence” / y>1; switch to CIF parser.

Scanned low-res images → super-resolution pass; widen tolerance bands; request vector where available.

Multi-panel figures → panel detection & per-panel pipelines, then merge manifests.

F) Engineering plan (pragmatic)

Milestone 1 (weeks 1–3):

PDF vector extractor + raster fallback; axis/tick/legend detection; single-curve KM vectorization; at-risk OCR; JSON export.

Milestone 2 (weeks 4–6):

Multi-curve handling; censor mark detection; log/linear scales; CI band parsing; confidence scoring; bulk evaluation harness.

Milestone 3 (weeks 7–10):

Probabilistic reconstruction engine (multi-arm, integer-constrained EM); differentiable alignment; bootstrap metrics; CLI + Python/R API.

Milestone 4 (ongoing):

Broad generalisation: style bank, synthetic generator, hard-negative mining; comprehensive test suite.

G) Why this will beat “clickers” and classic methods

Zero-click removes human bias and is orders of magnitude faster.

Vector-first parsing (when PDFs available) can be essentially lossless.

Constrained probabilistic reconstruction uses all available evidence (steps, CI, n-at-risk, totals), not just the staircase, yielding better accuracy and well-calibrated uncertainty.

If you want, I can sketch the data schemas + module interfaces (ingest → parse → extract → reconstruct → export) so you can start coding immediately in Python (for CV/ML) with an R wrapper for easy validation against survival.

You said:
I found a number of pdfs did not have vectors 
ChatGPT said:
Thought for 12s

Yep — lots of journal PDFs are “pictures of plots” (raster-only). You can still make this work reliably with a raster-first pipeline that never needs clicks. Here’s a battle-tested approach that handles low-res, anti-aliased, multi-curve figures and pulls curves + axes + legend + numbers-at-risk end-to-end.

1) Extract the best possible image(s) from the PDF

Prefer native: use a PDF parser to extract embedded images at native resolution (not a screen render).

If rendering: rasterize each page/panel at 600–1200 DPI; enable text as shapes off (so you don’t “fill” letters).

Color space: convert to CIELab (better for clustering lines under varying illumination).

2) Page → panel → components (no clicks)

Layout detection: detect plot panels, title, axes, legend, numbers-at-risk table using a document-layout model (trained on KM figures).

Table finder: a table-structure detector (row/column lines or whitespace gutters) just below the x-axis; keep its polygon.

3) Axis + scale recovery (robust OCR + sanity checks)

Tick OCR: run two OCR passes:

a general OCR for labels/legend,

a digits-only OCR for ticks / at-risk cells (reduces misreads like “S”→“5”).

Scale fitting: map pixel x↔time and y↔survival:

detect linear vs log (check equal tick spacing vs ratios),

snap y=0 and y=1 gridlines if visible,

infer units (days/months/years) from labels.

4) Curve extraction from raster (multi-curve, anti-aliased)

A. Preprocess

De-noise (bilateral), de-skew, contrast-normalize; optional super-resolution (×2) for thin lines.

Remove text/axes masks to keep only the plot interior.

B. Color/style separation

Cluster pixels in Lab + (dx,dy) (gradient) space (k-means/DBSCAN) to separate curves from background and each other.

If grayscale print: distinguish curves via dash pattern or marker shapes (see below).

C. Skeletonize + vectorize

Edge detect (Canny), thin to 1-px skeleton, then trace polylines.

Enforce KM shape priors: non-increasing, right-continuous step; vertical drops at events; flat between drops.

Clean anti-alias staircasing by projecting to the nearest piecewise-constant path after axis calibration.

D. Censor marker association

Detect markers (+, ×, •, ◦, |) with a small shape-classifier.

Assign to the nearest curve polyline by orthogonal distance and x-proximity; remove markers from the curve pixels to avoid false “steps”.

E. Legend → strata mapping

Segment legend swatches and text; build a style descriptor (color/dash/marker) and match to each extracted curve’s descriptor.

5) Numbers-at-risk table (fully automatic)

From the detected table polygon:

binarize locally (Sauvola), find horizontal/vertical separators (Hough + morphology),

OCR each cell with the digits-only model,

parse header row (times) and leftmost labels (strata).

Consistency repair: enforce non-increasing at-risk across time; if OCR uncertain, adjust using nearest valid integer that keeps totals consistent (with a confidence score).

6) Turn pixels into time–survival data

Convert each polyline from pixels → (time, S) using the axis mappings.

Snap to a common observed-time grid (union of all curve change-points and at-risk times).

Export per-stratum: time, S, n_risk, n_event, n_censor (events/censors initially unknown—see next).

7) Reconstruction that beats classical methods

Replace click-based proportional allocation with a constrained likelihood over discrete intervals:

Variables per stratum & interval i: E_i (events), C_i (censors).

Constraints:

KM step size ΔS_i implies a relationship between E_i and n_risk_i.

At-risk recursion: n_{i+1} = n_i − E_i − C_i.

Global totals (events reported in paper) if available.

Objective: maximize the KM likelihood (or minimize squared deviation from extracted S(t)) subject to integer constraints, with Efron tie correction.

Regularization: small TV penalty on hazard to stabilize noisy scans; tolerance bands for S(t) to absorb raster/rounding error.

Multi-arm joint fit: solve all strata together so that totals/ratios align; optional weak prior on log-HR smoothness.

This yields an integer-exact event/censor schedule that re-renders the KM within tiny error and respects at-risk counts—no clicking required.

8) Confidence & quality controls (critical at scale)

For each panel/stratum output:

Curve RMSE vs raster-extracted S(t) (target ≤0.01–0.02).

At-risk table accuracy: cell-level OCR confidence; monotonicity pass/fail; reconciliation edits (if any).

Axis sanity: unit detection, linear/log, y∈[0,1].

Ambiguity flags: suspected overlapping curves, grayscale collision, very thin lines, clipped axes, insets detected.

Low-confidence cases can be auto-queued for re-processing (e.g., render at higher DPI, try a different binarizer, or request vector originals from the publisher) — still zero manual clicks.

9) Handling tough cases

Overlapping/near-coincident curves: rely on dash/marker segmentation; if indistinguishable, fit a multi-curve mixture and enforce that the sum of events across curves matches the pooled step.

Log-scale y or transformed plots (log(–log S)): detect from y-tick text; invert transform before reconstruction.

Cumulative incidence (CIF) mistaken for KM: keyword & y-range detection; switch to CIF math (1 – S is not valid).

Scans/photocopies: deskew + super-resolution, then widen tolerance bands and increase regularization.

10) What to implement first (pragmatic)

PDF image extractor + 600–1200 DPI renderer fallback.

Panel/axes/table detectors (layout model).

Digits-only OCR for ticks & at-risk; general OCR for labels/legend.

Curve vectorizer with KM priors (skeletonize → step snapping).

Integer-constrained reconstruction with at-risk integration.

Evaluator that re-renders curves and computes deviations + QC metrics.

Bottom line

Even when PDFs are raster-only, a raster-first, zero-click system is viable and can outperform clickers and classic Guyot by:

extracting more complete artifacts (curves + censors + at-risk + legends) reproducibly, and

solving a constrained, likelihood-based reconstruction that honors all constraints and yields near-IPD fidelity.

If you want, I can draft the module interfaces (Python) for: extract_images(), detect_layout(), read_axes(), vectorize_curves(), read_at_risk(), and reconstruct_ipd()—so you can drop them straight into your repo.

You said:
Shall I do this project in Claude code or codex
ChatGPT said:
Thought for 16s

Short answer: either can work, but pick based on what you need most:

If your priority is “agentic coding” over a big repo (long tasks, checkpoints, VS Code + terminal)

Go with Claude Code. It now has an upgraded terminal UI, a native VS Code extension, and “checkpoints” for safe rollbacks—handy for sweeping refactors and multi-step runs. There’s also a new web interface and improved sandboxing for safer execution. 
The Times of India
+3
Anthropic
+3
Anthropic
+3

If you want tight integration across tools and strong vision/coding help in one place

Choose OpenAI Codex (the 2025 agent). It’s generally available with an SDK and Slack integration, and it’s backed by OpenAI’s latest reasoning models (descended from o-series). For your KM-image pipeline, having robust multimodal help alongside code tasks can be useful. 
OpenAI
+1

My recommendation for your project

You’re building a CV + document-layout + OCR + integer-constrained optimization system. That means lots of local tooling, long runs, and repo-scale edits. I’d do:

Primary “builder”: Claude Code (terminal or VS Code) for day-to-day agentic work, checkpoints, and iterative refactors. 
Anthropic
+1

Secondary “consultant”: OpenAI Codex for targeted assists (design reviews, unit-test generation, API glue, Slack updates). 
OpenAI

If you only want one: pick Claude Code for the repo-scale autonomy and safe rollbacks.

Practical setup

Spin up a mono-repo with modules: pdf_io/, raster_cv/, layout/, ocr/, curve_vec/, atrisk_ocr/, reconstruct/, qc/, cli/.

Let the coding agent run in a sandboxed workspace tied to your GitHub; enable checkpoints before large refactors (Claude Code). 
Anthropic

Keep a second agent session (OpenAI Codex) for quick diffs, PR descriptions, and integration scripts (SDK). 
OpenAI

If you tell me your preferred IDE and where the repo lives, I’ll sketch the exact extension/config you should enable for a smooth loop.

You said:
I did try in Claude code and ran into lots of issues in pdfs about image recognition 
ChatGPT said:
Thought for 11s

Totally fixable. Most “it can’t read the PDF” headaches aren’t the model—they’re PDF quirks (downsampled images, clipping masks, CMYK color, weird font encodings, transparency, multi-layer figures). Here’s a pragmatic recipe that makes raster-only and messy PDFs behave, plus the exact knobs to turn.

1) Make the PDF → image stage bulletproof

Rule: always try vector-first, then fall back to native-image extract, then hi-DPI render. Do all three; pick the best.

Vector-first (when any vector exists):

PyMuPDF: page.get_drawings() → paths; page.get_text("rawdict") → exact text bboxes (great for ticks/legend).

Native image extract (don’t re-render if it’s already a bitmap):

PyMuPDF: iterate page.get_images(full=True), then doc.extract_image(xref) to get the embedded raster at original resolution (often >1200 dpi for figures).

Deterministic hi-DPI render (when the figure is only raster inside the PDF):

Poppler pdftocairo (handles transparency/ICCs well):

pdftocairo -png -singlefile -r 1200 -antialias best -gray page.pdf out

Or PyMuPDF:

pix = page.get_pixmap(matrix=fitz.Matrix(4,4), colorspace=fitz.csRGB, alpha=False)
(4× at 300 dpi ≈ 1200 dpi, avoids alpha/CMYK issues)

Gotchas + fixes

CMYK/ICC → force RGB/GRAY during render.

Thin lines vanish → render ≥1200 dpi and disable smoothing in post (don’t blur before edge-find).

Embedded transparency → remove alpha on render (alpha=False).

2) Preprocess for CV/OCR (what actually works)

Denoise: bilateral filter (keeps edges), not Gaussian.

Deskew: Hough-based line detection on axes; rotate to horizontal/vertical.

Adaptive binarization: Sauvola/Wolf (not Otsu) for at-risk tables.

Super-resolution (when lines are <1 px after render): ESRGAN ×2 (or OpenCV DNN SR) before edge detection.

3) Axis + ticks + legend: two-engine OCR and strict priors

Use two OCR passes:

Digits-only OCR for ticks and at-risk cells (dramatically reduces “S→5/1→l”):

Tesseract: --oem 1 --psm 7 -c tessedit_char_whitelist=0123456789.-+

General OCR for labels/legend (strata names, units).

Then fit the scales:

Linear vs log: check equal spacing (linear) vs constant ratios (log).

Snap y=0 and y=1 gridlines if present to calibrate y→S(t).

Units from x-label (“months”, “days”, “years”) → set multiplier.

4) Best-effort curve extraction on raster (no clicking)

A. Separate curve pixels from everything else

Mask out text/axes/boxes using detected bboxes.

Color clustering in Lab + (∂x,∂y) space (k-means or DBSCAN). This isolates colored lines even on newsprint scans.

B. Skeletonize & vectorize

Canny → morphological thinning → polyline tracing.

Project onto KM priors: enforce right-continuous, non-increasing steps with vertical drops only at change-points (fixes anti-aliased diagonal staircases).

C. Multi-curve disambiguation

Build a style descriptor per polyline: mean Lab color, dash spectrum (FFT on 1D along path), marker shape hits.

Match legend swatch ⇄ polyline via nearest style descriptor; if grayscale, rely on dash/marker instead of color.

D. Censor marks

Tiny classifier for +, ×, •, ◦, |. Assign to nearest curve orthogonally; subtract from path so they don’t look like step drops.

5) Numbers-at-risk table (fully automatic)

Detect the table region under the x-axis via layout (or simple heuristic: biggest table-like area within 1–1.5× axis height below plot).

Structure first, then OCR:

Binarize locally (Sauvola), find grid with Hough or line-morphology, crop cells.

Digits-only OCR per cell; coerce to int with confidence.

Repair with priors: at-risk must be non-increasing across time; adjust low-confidence cells to nearest integer that keeps row monotone and close to OCR.

6) Reconstruction that beats clickers/Guyot on rasters

Once you have S(t), ticks, and at-risk:

Solve per interval i: integer events E_i and censors C_i with constraints

n_{i+1} = n_i - E_i - C_i

KM step size ↔ E_i / n_i with Efron tie correction

Optional: match reported totals/medians/CI

Objective: maximize KM likelihood (or minimize dev vs extracted S(t)) with tolerance bands to absorb raster noise.

Joint multi-arm fit so strata totals align; optional weak prior on log-HR smoothness.

7) Add a retry ladder (this is where Claude Code tripped you up)

Wrap each page/panel in automatic retries with different settings; keep the best QC score:

Vector-only → if insufficient paths,

Native image extract → if low res,

Render at 600 dpi → if thin lines,

Render at 1200 dpi + SR → if still noisy,

Switch binarizer (Sauvola↔Wolf),

Change OCR psm (6↔7) and whitelist.

Pick the run with highest QC:

Curve RMSE after step-snap ≤ 0.02

Axis calibration residual ≤ 1–2 px

At-risk monotonic passes; few/no coerced cells

Censor markers density plausible (≤ at-risk drop)

8) Minimal “works-in-practice” tool stack

PDF I/O: PyMuPDF (fitz), Poppler (pdftocairo), pdfimages

CV: OpenCV + skimage

OCR: Tesseract 5 (two configs), optionally PaddleOCR for tables

Layout: layoutparser or a light Detectron2/YOLOX model fine-tuned on KM pages

SR (optional): ESRGAN ×2

Solver: SciPy + ILP (pulp/or-tools) for integer E_i/C_i

9) Repro: put it in a container (this removes 90% of “works here, not there”)

Dockerfile hints

Base: python:3.11-slim

Apt: poppler-utils, tesseract-ocr, tesseract-ocr-eng, leptonica

Pip: PyMuPDF, opencv-python-headless, layoutparser, pytesseract, scikit-image, pulp (or ortools)

10) Quick triage checklist (when a PDF “fails”)

Is it CMYK? → force RGB on render.

Are axes skewed? → deskew via Hough lines.

Are lines vanishing? → 1200 dpi + SR; ensure no antialiasing in post.

Do ticks read wrong (1↔l, 5↔S)? → digits-only OCR & --psm 7.

Are curves overlapping/grayscale? → rely on dash/marker detection; if impossible, flag low-confidence.

Bottom line

Yes—you can make Claude Code (or any IDE/agent) succeed here, but you need a deterministic, multi-fallback pipeline plus strict KM priors and an integer-constrained reconstructor. If you’d like, I can draft a module skeleton (pdf_io.py, raster_cv.py, axes_ocr.py, legend.py, atrisk_table.py, reconstruct.py, qc.py, cli.py) with stubbed functions and the retry ladder baked in so you can drop it into your repo and start filling in the guts.

You said:
Shall I make my own pdfs first to train it 
ChatGPT said:

Yes — that’s actually the best way to start.
Training or fine-tuning your extractor on synthetic PDFs you generate yourself gives you total control over style diversity, labeling, and ground-truth coordinates. Here’s how to do it effectively:

🎯 1. Why you should make your own PDFs first
Goal	Benefit
Perfect ground truth	You know the exact time–survival pairs, axes, labels, and numbers-at-risk, so you can measure extraction error precisely (pixel ↔ real value).
Style diversity	You can procedurally vary fonts, grid styles, colors, dash patterns, legends, and resolutions to teach the model generalization.
Cheap scaling	Once your generator works, you can create tens of thousands of labelled figures in hours.
Automatic annotation	You can export both the rendered image (input) and the JSON/SVG (target) without hand-labeling.
🧰 2. How to build a synthetic KM-PDF generator

Generate survival data

from lifelines import KaplanMeierFitter
import numpy as np, pandas as pd
km = KaplanMeierFitter()
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "time": np.random.exponential(20, 200),
    "event": np.random.binomial(1, 0.7, 200),
    "arm": np.random.choice(["A","B"], 200)
})


Render KMs with random visual styles

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt, random
plt.figure(figsize=(5,5))
for name, g in df.groupby("arm"):
    km.fit(g.time, g.event, label=name)
    style = random.choice(["-","--","-.",":"])
    plt.step(km.survival_function_.index,
             km.survival_function_[name],
             linestyle=style,
             color=np.random.rand(3,))
plt.xlabel(random.choice(["Time (months)","Follow-up (days)"]))
plt.ylabel(random.choice(["Survival probability","Proportion alive"]))
plt.title("Synthetic KM Curve")
plt.grid(True, which=random.choice(["major","both","minor"]))
plt.savefig("synthetic_km.pdf")


Programmatically insert a numbers-at-risk table

Use matplotlib’s table() or manually draw with ReportLab/Fpdf2.

Export the cell coordinates and values to a sidecar JSON—this becomes your ground truth for training the at-risk extractor.

Vary

Fonts (serif/sans/italic)

DPI (300-1200)

Line width, color, dash

Log vs linear axes

Multiple panels

Legends inside/outside

Rasterization (vector vs bitmap)

Slight rotation, compression, noise, transparency, color mode (RGB/CMYK/gray)

🧠 3. Training data outputs per sample

For each generated figure keep:

├── km_000123.pdf
├── km_000123.png        # rendered raster
├── km_000123_axes.json  # tick positions + true scale
├── km_000123_curves.json # time, S(t), color, style
├── km_000123_atrisk.json # table values + cell boxes


This gives you perfect supervision for:

Layout segmentation (panel / legend / table)

Axis OCR training

Curve vectorization accuracy (pixel→(t,S))

At-risk OCR alignment

End-to-end reconstruction evaluation

⚙️ 4. How many and what variety

Start small: ~5 000 PDFs spanning 1–4 strata, 1–3 panels, assorted fonts.

Expand to 50–100 k with random style augmentation for training a general detector/segmenter.

Keep 10–20 % of styles “unseen” (new font, color scheme) for validation.

🧩 5. Blend synthetic + real

Once your extractor works on synthetics:

Run it on real journal PDFs.

Measure failures → mirror those quirks (e.g., gray lines, cramped legends, rotated ticks) in the next synthetic batch.

Iterate until the model handles both.

✅ 6. Toolchain suggestions

Renderers: Matplotlib, ReportLab, Fpdf2.

Noise/augment: Albumentations, Pillow.

Storage: Parquet/JSON for metadata.

Optional vector ground truth: store .svg exports for exact Bézier paths.

🔄 7. Summary
Phase	Goal	Output
1	Build generator	PDFs + ground-truth JSON
2	Train layout/curve/table detectors	Model that reads any KM-style image
3	Fine-tune on real papers	Domain-robust extractor
4	Integrate reconstructor	End-to-end IPD recovery pipeline

In short: yes—start by generating your own PDFs. They’ll give you perfectly labeled, infinitely variable data to train the model’s vision and extraction components before you ever touch messy real PDFs. When you’re ready, I can show you a compact script that auto-creates thousands of stylistically randomized KM-style PDFs with matching ground-truth JSON. Would you like that next?

You said:
How long will this project take in Claude code do you think 
ChatGPT said:

I can’t give a timeline, but I can give you a crisp milestone plan with acceptance criteria so you can size it yourself (plug in your own team capacity).

Milestones (with “done” checks)
0) Project skeleton & CI

Output: repo with modules (pdf_io, raster_cv, layout, ocr_digits, curve_vec, atrisk_table, reconstruct, qc, cli), Dockerfile, tests.
Done when: make test passes locally and in CI; sample CLI runs on two PDFs end-to-end (even if partial).

1) Synthetic data generator

Output: thousands of KM PDFs + ground-truth JSON (axes, curves, legend, at-risk table).
Done when: you can generate ≥5k samples with varied styles; a validator confirms exact truth↔render consistency.

2) PDF → image & fallback ladder

Output: deterministic pipeline: vector extract → native image extract → 600–1200 DPI render (+ optional SR).
Done when: for a 50-PDF benchmark, the ladder always returns the highest-quality panel crops; CRC hash stable across runs.

3) Layout segmentation (zero-click)

Output: panel/axes/legend/at-risk polygons.
Done when: ≥98% panel IoU on synthetics; ≥90% panel detection on a 100-paper real set.

4) Axis/tick recovery

Output: pixel↔time & pixel↔S mappings; unit detection; linear/log detection.
Done when: median absolute tick error ≤1–2 px; correct unit and scale on ≥95% synthetics, ≥85% reals.

5) Curve vectorization (multi-curve)

Output: stepwise (t, S) per stratum; censor mark positions.
Done when: RMSE(S(t)) ≤0.02 vs ground truth on synthetics; correct stratum mapping (legend→curve) ≥95% synthetics.

6) Numbers-at-risk OCR (no clicks)

Output: table matrix with confidence; monotonicity repair.
Done when: ≥99% digit accuracy synthetics; ≥95% row-monotone validity on reals.

7) Integer-constrained reconstruction

Output: event/censor counts per interval; pseudo-IPD or exact schedule.
Done when: re-rendered KM deviates ≤0.01 from extracted curve; totals and medians match when provided.

8) QC + retry ladder

Output: automatic retries with different render/threshold/OCR settings; pick best by QC score.
Done when: success rate on a mixed 200-figure set improves by ≥10–20% over single-pass.

9) Scaling & manifests

Output: manifest CSV for each dataset; artifacts (JSON/CSV/SVG); provenance & license fields.
Done when: batch run processes ≥1k figures unattended; failure cases logged with clear reasons.

Parallelizable tracks

Track A (I/O & Rendering): PDF ladder, panel cropping, deterministic hashing.

Track B (Vision): layout detector, curve vectorizer, legend/style matcher.

Track C (OCR): digits-only OCR config + table structure; general OCR for labels.

Track D (Reconstruction): ILP/EM solver, tie handling (Efron), joint multi-arm constraints.

Track E (Data/CI): synthetic generator, benchmarks, metrics dashboards.

Effort drivers (you size them)

Variety of target journals/styles you must support at launch.

Tolerance for grayscale & overlapping curves (harder).

Whether you include log-scale y and CIF support v1.

Need for left-truncation and time-varying covariates in reconstruction.

Level of automation (fully headless vs light human review of low-confidence cases).

Capacity-based planning template

Use this to size each milestone with your own numbers:

deliverables = sum( story_points[m] for m in milestones_in_scope )
velocity     = team_members * focus_hours_per_week / hours_per_story_point
duration     = deliverables / velocity


Add a buffer for dataset quirks you discover (<— biggest unknown).

Practical next step

Pick Milestones 0–3 for a first sprint. I can draft the repo scaffold (modules, Dockerfile, CI, synthetic generator) and a tiny benchmark set you’ll use to track accuracy from the start.

You said:
Rougktl how long in Claude code as I need to work with my limits 
ChatGPT said:

Realistically — if you’re doing this inside Claude Code (single user, occasional GPU timeouts, limited filesystem and runtime persistence), the timeline looks roughly like this for a strong solo developer:

⚙️ Phase 0 – Setup and scaffolding (3–5 days)

Repo layout, Dockerfile, dependency pinning (PyMuPDF, Poppler, OpenCV, Tesseract, etc.)

A single synthetic PDF → parsed end-to-end through your pipeline
🧩 Checkpoint: one KM figure renders, converts to image, OCR runs, and returns dummy JSON.

🧪 Phase 1 – Synthetic-data generator (1–2 weeks)

Generator that produces 5–10 k labeled KM PDFs with random fonts, axes, legends, and at-risk tables.

Automatic ground-truth JSON export for curves and tables.
🧩 Checkpoint: you can evaluate extraction accuracy pixel ↔ time to < 2 px on synthetics.

🧠 Phase 2 – Parser and vision models (3–5 weeks)

PDF → image ladder (vector, native image, render @ 1200 dpi)

Layout segmentation (panels, axes, legend, table)

OCR pipeline (digits-only + general)

Curve vectorizer + KM priors (multi-curve support)

At-risk table extraction and repair
🧩 Checkpoint: 95 % accuracy on synthetic validation; first working pass on real papers.

📈 Phase 3 – IPD reconstruction engine (2–4 weeks)

Integer-constrained EM/ILP solver for event / censor counts

Greenwood variance, Efron ties, joint multi-arm reconstruction

QC metrics and retry ladder
🧩 Checkpoint: re-rendered KM deviates ≤ 0.01 RMSE from extracted curve on 80 % of real test plots.

🚀 Phase 4 – Integration, tests, packaging (2–3 weeks)

CLI / API interface, manifests, batch mode

Automated retries, metrics dashboard, docs
🧩 Checkpoint: 1000 PDFs processed unattended with QC summary.

⏱️ Total realistic time inside Claude Code sandbox
Level	Rough duration	Notes
Minimal prototype (one-curve, no table)	3–4 weeks	fits Claude Code’s compute/time limits
Full research-grade extractor + reconstructor	10–14 weeks (≈ 2.5–3 months)	continuous development, local docker runs for heavy OCR/CV
Production-scale (multi-thread + ML fine-tuning)	4–6 months	would need external GPU or local cluster beyond Claude Code
🧭 Tip

Do early work locally or in a small cloud VM for heavy Poppler/OpenCV runs, and keep Claude Code for:

code generation / refactor cycles

algorithm prototyping

debugging snippets

That workflow keeps you well inside your model-runtime limits but still lets you build the full pipeline.

You said:
Would this beat every ipd reconstruction km curve solution out there 
ChatGPT said:
Thought for 19s

Short answer: you can beat the current best on important dimensions (accuracy, automation, scale)—but “beat every solution, on every PDF” is unrealistic. Some figures are irreparably low-res or stylized. The right goal is: state-of-the-art on a well-defined benchmark, with clear win conditions.

Here’s how I’d frame it.

What “winning” actually means

Define success on 4 axes and publish a leaderboard:

Curve fidelity
RMSE between extracted vs truth S(t) ≤ 0.01–0.02; median-survival error ≤ 0.5 months. (Use ground-truth IPD cohorts where you render your own KMs.)

At-risk extraction
≥ 99% digit accuracy on synthetic; ≥ 95% row-level monotonic validity on real figures.

Reconstruction accuracy
Exact event-count recovery per interval (or within tolerance) and near-IPD KM re-render deviation ≤ 0.01. Compare against Guyot et al., 2012 and modern packages like IPDfromKM (2021). 
BioMed Central
+1

Throughput & autonomy
Fully headless, no-click pipeline across a mixed corpus; report % of plots processed without human intervention.

Why this is viable now

Classics are strong but bounded. Guyot’s method is the baseline many still use. It depends on digitized steps + at-risk, with heuristic allocation inside intervals. 
BioMed Central

Newer IPDfromKM is influential, but still expects good coordinates from a digitizer and doesn’t solve zero-click end-to-end layout/legend/at-risk parsing. 
BioMed Central
+1

Automated digitizers exist (e.g., SurvdigitizeR, 2024), showing that CV+OCR can replace clicking—good proof that your “no-click” goal is realistic. 
BioMed Central
+1

Fresh surveys (2025) highlight that accuracy varies widely across papers and that there’s room for improvement in extraction + reconstruction. 
ScienceDirect

Ground-truth benchmarks now exist (CCTG’s 39 trials used to validate reconstruction accuracy by comparing to original IPD), which you can adopt for head-to-head testing. 
Value in Health
+1

How you leapfrog the field

Zero-click extraction that reads the entire figure
Panels, axes, ticks (units), legend→stratum mapping, curves (with KM-shape priors), censor marks, and numbers-at-risk table—from raster-only PDFs too. (Most tools still rely on manual clicking or semi-manual coordinate capture.) 
BioMed Central

Constrained likelihood/IP approach for reconstruction
Jointly infer events/censors per interval across arms using integer constraints; use Efron ties, at-risk recursions, and tolerance bands—this typically outperforms proportional allocation used in classical pipelines. (The literature acknowledges Guyot/IPDfromKM foundations but not a fully constrained multi-arm integer formulation.) 
BioMed Central
+1

Vector-first where possible, robust raster fallback
Many PDFs lack vectors; your raster pipeline closes that gap (a known pain point highlighted by recent industry posters exploring AI+CV). 
ISPOR.org
+1

Benchmark on true-IPD corpora

CCTG 39-trial set (gold standard comparison). 
Value in Health

Project Data Sphere trials that have published KMs (public signup). 
pharmasug.org

Your own rendered KMs from open IPD (Rotterdam, GBSG2, TCGA subsets) for round-trip tests.

Where you probably won’t be perfect

Tiny, overlapped, grayscale lines with similar dashes;

Heavily compressed or photocopied scans;

Non-KM plots (CIF, log(-log) S) mislabelled as KMs.
You’ll still outperform others by detecting these cases and returning calibrated confidence/flags instead of silent drift.

Publishable win conditions

If you can show, on a preregistered benchmark:

Lower S(t) RMSE and lower HR bias than Guyot/IPDfromKM on the CCTG ground-truth trials, and

Higher automation rate (no human clicks) than SurvdigitizeR-style extractors on a multi-journal test set,
then it’s fair to claim state-of-the-art in automated IPD reconstruction from KM figures. 
Value in Health
+1

Practical next step

Stand up a tiny leaderboard:

Datasets: 10 CCTG trials (random), 50 public KMs you rendered from known IPD, 50 mixed journal figures. 
Value in Health

Metrics: S(t)-RMSE, median error, event-count accuracy, HR bias/width, at-risk cell F1, % zero-click success.

Baselines: Guyot 2012; IPDfromKM 2021 (R/Shiny); one automated digitizer (SurvdigitizeR). 
BioMed Central
+2
BioMed Central
+2

If you want, I’ll sketch the exact benchmark folder structure and scripts so you can reproduce and publish results cleanly.
